lenses.guidance: The name Israel (v 28) puns on sarah ('to strive') with El; Peniel/Penuel (vv 30-31) means 'face of God'. Hebrew wordplay binds limp, name, and place into a single etymological scar — a covenant identity marked by struggle.

Mahanaim (v 2) and Peniel (v 30) bookend the chapter — angelic encounter at the threshold, divine wrestler at the river. The structure frames Jacob's last night as a liminal crossing where a name and a body are remade.

The night-wrestler at the Jabbok (vv 24-30) prefigures Gethsemane's struggle: a son who pleads, a body marked by the encounter, a blessing carried through wound. Israel's limp foreshadows the pattern of victory through scar.

Jacob's prayer at vv 9-12 names the covenant promise back to God before the wrestling. The name change to Israel (v 28) marks the redemptive turn: the supplanter is reconstituted as the people through whom the promise will run.

Hosea 12:3-4 reads vv 24-30 as Jacob 'striving' yet 'weeping' for grace — an arc completed in Christ, who wrestled in Gethsemane and prevailed through the cross, securing the blessing his people could only beg for.

'I will not let you go unless you bless me' (v 26) defines persevering prayer. The dawn-blessing comes with a permanent limp (v 31) — God-given dependence, not God-given strength, becomes Israel's mark. Trust costs.
